---
title: "Lesson 8: Distributions of Sums"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_distributions-of-sums.mp4 >}}

[transcript](01_distributions-of-sums.en.txt){target="_blank"}
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-distributions-of-sums_module1_lesson7.pdf){width=700 height=400}
:::

## Distributions of Sums {#sec-distributions-of-sums}

Let $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{Bernoulli}(p),$ and define $Y=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.$

Each $X_i$ equals $1$ with probability $p$ and $0$ with probability $1-p$. 

Therefore, $Y$ counts the total number of successes in $n$ independent trials, so $Y \sim \operatorname{Binomial}(n,p)$

Not every sum of random variables has such an immediate interpretation. 

Moment generating functions provide a systematic method for identifying their distributions.

## Moment Generating Functions {#sec-moment-generating-functions}

::: {#def-moment-generating-function .definition}
## Moment generating function

The moment generating function [moment generating function]{.aside} (MGF) of a random variable $X$ is

$$
M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{tX}\right] \qquad
$$ {#eq-mgf-definition}

for values of $t$ at which the expectation exists.

For a continuous random variable,

$$
M_X(t)
=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
e^{tx}f_X(x)\,dx,
$$

whereas for a discrete random variable,

$$
M_X(t)
=
\sum_x e^{tx}P(X=x).
$$
:::

The MGF generates the raw moments of $X$. When differentiation under the expectation is valid,

$$
M_X^{(k)}(0)
=
\mathbb{E}[X^k],
\qquad
k=1,2,3,\ldots.
$$

::: {#thm-mgf-uniqueness .theorem}
## Uniqueness of moment generating functions

Suppose the MGFs of $X$ and $Y$ exist on an open interval containing $0$. If

$$
M_X(t)
=
M_Y(t)
$$

throughout that interval, then $X$ and $Y$ have the same distribution.
:::

Thus, once the MGF of a sum is recognized as the MGF of a known distribution, the distribution of the sum is identified.

## Example: Bernoulli MGF {#sec-bernoulli-mgf}

::: {#exm-bernoulli-mgf .example}
## Bernoulli distribution

Let

$$
X
\sim
\operatorname{Bernoulli}(p).
$$

Since $X\in\{0,1\}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t)
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{tX}\right] \\
&=
\sum_x e^{tx}P(X=x) \\
&=
e^{0t}P(X=0)
+
e^{1t}P(X=1) \\
&=
1\cdot(1-p)
+
e^t p \\
&=
1-p+pe^t.
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

## Example: Binomial MGF {#sec-binomial-mgf}

::: {#exm-binomial-mgf .example}
## Binomial distribution

Let

$$
X
\sim
\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p).
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t)
&=
\sum_{x=0}^n
e^{tx}P(X=x) \\
&=
\sum_{x=0}^n
e^{tx}
\binom{n}{x}
p^x(1-p)^{n-x} \\
&=
\sum_{x=0}^n
\binom{n}{x}
\left(pe^t\right)^x
(1-p)^{n-x} \\
&=
\left(pe^t+1-p\right)^n
\qquad \text{by the binomial theorem}.
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

The binomial theorem used here is

$$
(a+b)^n
=
\sum_{k=0}^n
\binom{n}{k}
a^k b^{n-k}.
$$

## MGF of a Sum of Independent Random Variables {#sec-mgf-independent-sum}

::: {#thm-mgf-independent-sum .theorem}
## MGF of an independent sum

Let $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ be independent random variables and define

$$
Y
=
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.
$$

Then

$$
M_Y(t)
=
\prod_{i=1}^n M_{X_i}(t),
$$

for values of $t$ at which the MGFs exist.
:::

::: {#prf-mgf-independent-sum .proof}
## Proof

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Y(t)
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{tY}\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[
e^{t\sum_{i=1}^n X_i}
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[
\prod_{i=1}^n e^{tX_i}
\right] \\
&=
\prod_{i=1}^n
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{tX_i}\right]
\qquad \text{by independence} \\
&=
\prod_{i=1}^n M_{X_i}(t).
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

In particular, if

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} X,
$$

then

$$
M_Y(t)
=
\left[M_X(t)\right]^n.
$$

## Example: Sum of Bernoulli Random Variables {#sec-sum-bernoulli}

::: {#exm-sum-bernoulli .example}
## Bernoulli sum

Suppose

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Bernoulli}(p),
$$

and let

$$
Y
=
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.
$$

The MGF of each summand is

$$
M_{X_i}(t)
=
1-p+pe^t.
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Y(t)
&=
\left[M_{X_1}(t)\right]^n \\
&=
\left(1-p+pe^t\right)^n.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the MGF of a $\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p)$ random variable. Hence

$$
Y
\sim
\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p).
$$
:::

## Example: Exponential MGF {#sec-exponential-mgf}

::: {#exm-exponential-mgf .example}
## Exponential distribution

Let

$$
X
\sim
\operatorname{Exp}(\text{rate}=\lambda),
$$

with density

$$
f_X(x)
=
\lambda e^{-\lambda x}
\mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x).
$$

For $t<\lambda$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t)
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{tX}\right] \\
&=
\int_0^\infty
e^{tx}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx \\
&=
\int_0^\infty
\lambda e^{-(\lambda-t)x}\,dx \\
&=
\frac{\lambda}{\lambda-t}
\int_0^\infty
(\lambda-t)e^{-(\lambda-t)x}\,dx \\
&=
\frac{\lambda}{\lambda-t}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The integral in the penultimate line equals $1$ because its integrand is an exponential density with rate $\lambda-t$.
:::

## Example: Gamma MGF {#sec-gamma-mgf}

::: {#exm-gamma-mgf .example}
## Gamma distribution

Let

$$
X
\sim
\Gamma(\alpha,\beta),
$$

where $\alpha$ is the shape parameter and $\beta$ is the rate parameter. Its density is

$$
f_X(x)
=
\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}
\mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x).
$$

For $t<\beta$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t)
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{tX}\right] \\
&=
\int_0^\infty
e^{tx}
\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}\,dx \\
&=
\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
\int_0^\infty
x^{\alpha-1}e^{-(\beta-t)x}\,dx \\
&=
\frac{\beta^\alpha}{(\beta-t)^\alpha}
\int_0^\infty
\frac{(\beta-t)^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
x^{\alpha-1}e^{-(\beta-t)x}\,dx \\
&=
\left(
\frac{\beta}{\beta-t}
\right)^\alpha.
\end{aligned}
$$

The final integral equals $1$ because its integrand is the density of a $\Gamma(\alpha,\beta-t)$ random variable.
:::

## Example: Sum of Exponential Random Variables {#sec-sum-exponential}

::: {#exm-sum-exponential .example}
## Exponential sum

Suppose

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\text{rate}=\lambda),
$$

and define

$$
Y
=
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.
$$

Since

$$
M_{X_i}(t)
=
\frac{\lambda}{\lambda-t},
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Y(t)
&=
\left[M_{X_1}(t)\right]^n \\
&=
\left(
\frac{\lambda}{\lambda-t}
\right)^n.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the MGF of the $\Gamma(n,\lambda)$ distribution. Therefore,

$$
Y
\sim
\Gamma(n,\lambda).
$$

Thus, the sum of $n$ independent and identically distributed exponential random variables with rate $\lambda$ has a gamma distribution with shape $n$ and rate $\lambda$.
:::

## Common Distributions of Sums {#sec-common-distributions-of-sums}

The preceding results and related closure properties can be summarized as follows.

### Bernoulli and binomial sums {#sec-bernoulli-binomial-sums}

If

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Bernoulli}(p),
$$

then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
\sim
\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p).
$$

If

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_m
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p),
$$

then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^m X_i
\sim
\operatorname{Binomial}(mn,p).
$$

### Exponential and gamma sums {#sec-exponential-gamma-sums}

If

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\text{rate}=\lambda),
$$

then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
\sim
\Gamma(n,\lambda).
$$

If

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\Gamma(\alpha,\beta),
$$

with a common rate $\beta$, then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
\sim
\Gamma(n\alpha,\beta).
$$

### Normal sums {#sec-normal-sums}

If

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
\sim
\mathcal{N}(n\mu,n\sigma^2).
$$

More generally, if $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are independent and

$$
X_i
\sim
\mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\sigma_i^2),
$$

then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
\sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\sum_{i=1}^n \mu_i,
\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma_i^2
\right).
$$

For constants $a_1,\ldots,a_n$, the weighted sum

$$
Y
=
\sum_{i=1}^n a_iX_i
$$

satisfies

$$
Y
\sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\mu_i,
\sum_{i=1}^n a_i^2\sigma_i^2
\right).
$$



### Reflection

I remember that in my first course on probability theory by professor [ely merzbach](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=waNmBS8AAAAJ&hl=en) pointed out how sums and products of random variables do not typically result in random variables of the same distribution family. He was so happy when he gave us a number of examples. However at that point the distribution he gave us meant very little to me. 

By the time I was actually using these concepts in practice I had more or less forgotten this important lesson. This is a **common pitfall** particularly for people who lack training in probability theory.

I recently reviewed his [book](https://www.magnespress.co.il/en/book/Probability_Theory-2784) @Merzbach99 and found myself paying more and more attention to the material on characteristic functions. 

These are perhaps the most powerful tool in probability theory for deriving distributions of sums and products of random variables. They are however complex valued functions and require some knowledge of complex analysis as well as the convolution operation. However this not only simplifies many derivations and proofs but provides a link to the powerful tools of Fourier analysis that are widely used in signal processing and machine learning.

I hope, some day soon I will write a blog post on this topic! However just building an intuition about the convolution operation requires much ground work.
